entry.title: Scientific American: July 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Record-Breaking Voyager Spacecraft Begin to Power Down
  • Subverting Climate Science in the Classroom
  • How Parents' Trauma Leaves Biological Traces in Children
  • Toxic Slime Contributed to Earth's Worst Mass Extinction--And It's Making a Comeback
  • Astronomers Gear Up to Grapple with the High-Tension Cosmos
  • 'Momentum Computing' Pushes Technology's Thermodynamic Limits
  • Students Deserve to Learn about the Climate Emergency
  • Readers Respond to the March 2022 Issue
  • How Connected Cars Can Map Urban Heat Islands
  • Marker Tip--Without Ink!--Makes a Hardy Medical Sampler
  • New Satellite Is a 'Swiss Army Knife' in Space
  • Dinosaur Diets May Help Explain Dramatic Diversity
  • How Antarctic Krill Coordinate the Biggest Swarms in the World
  • These Spiders Spring Off Their Mates to Avoid Sexual Cannibalism
  • 'Frog Skin' Cell Type Found in Mammal Mouths
  • News Briefs from around the World: July 2022
  • Venomous Snail Unlocks New Diabetes Drugs
  • See the Strange Underground Detector Probing Neutrino Mysteries
  • Skies Are Sucking More Water from the Land
  • How Snakes Breathe while Crushing Prey
  • Crowdfunding Isn't Enough in a Crisis
  • Why Do Mental Illnesses--From Depression to Schizophrenia--Raise the Risk of Dementia?
  • We Need to Make 'Electrifying Everything' Easier
  • Climate Destroyers Go to Jail, Martian Travel Guide, Bee Interiority, and More
  • 50, 100 & 150 Years Ago: July 2022
  • Wildfires Followed by Severe Rain Will Become More Common
  • Poem: 'On Visible Light'
  • Why Kids Are Afraid to Ask for Help
  • Science Needs to Shrink Its Carbon Footprint
Popular Science. Monthly magazine. Should be downloaded around the middle of each month.

entry.title: New Yorker: July 11 & 18, 2022
entry.content:

Articles of the week's New Yorker magazine

entry.title: The Third Pole: 12 Jul, 2022
entry.summary: The Third Pole: 12 Jul, 2022 published at 2022-07-13 09:36AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Heatwave in Kashmir spells difficult future for pastoralists
  • How plastic is fuelling a hidden climate crisis in Southeast Asia
  • Women’s lives get harder in Bundelkhand as water scarcity grows
  • Stalled monsoon behind devastating floods in northeast India and Bangladesh
  • In Vietnam’s Mekong Delta, sand mining means lost homes and fortunes
  • How much progress has been made on Kyrgyz-Uzbek water cooperation?
  • Bonn climate talks end with ‘almost empty pages’
The Third Pole is a multilingual platform dedicated to promoting information and discussion about the Himalayan watershed and the rivers that originate there.

entry.title: The Diplomat: 13 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Chinese FM Pledges Progress on South China Sea Code of Conduct
  • Who Gets to Speak for Migrant Workers in Singapore?
  • What the Roe Reversal Means for Abortion Rights in Vietnam

  • Mongolia: Wrestling With Modernization

  • ‘Hopeless’: Japan’s Weak Opposition No Match for Ruling LDP

The Diplomat is a current-affairs magazine for the Asia-Pacific, with news and analysis on politics, security, business, technology and life across the region.

entry.title: ProPublica: 12 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • School Board Candidates Who Criticized the Hiring of a Black DEI Educator Lose Their Elections
  • Ten Ways Billionaires Avoid Taxes on an Epic Scale
ProPublica is an independent, nonprofit newsroom that produces investigative journalism with moral force.

entry.title: Nautilus: 8 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:

  • Gravitational Waves Continue to Astound

  • Mysteries Are to Be Embraced, But Also to Be Solved
  • How Are the Bees?

  • Who Are the Scientists Here?
  • The Giraffe Neck Evolved for Sexual Combat
  • Print Edition 43
  • Print Edition 42
  • Print Edition 41
  • Print Edition 40
  • Life Helps Make Almost Half of All Minerals on Earth
  • Under Anesthesia, Where Do Our Minds Go?
  • Unlocking Mom’s Brain
  • Thinking Like a Scientist Will Make You Happier
  • Don’t Give Up on Facts
  • Are You a Naïve Realist?
  • Moving Beyond Mimicry in Artificial Intelligence
  • Overthrowing the Patriarchy Through Ecstatic Sex
  • What Oceanographers Can Learn From Their Animal Colleagues
  • The Secrets of the Blind Salamander
Nautilus is a different kind of science magazine. Our stories take you into the depths of science and spotlight its ripples in our lives and cultures. We believe any subject in science, no matter how complex, can be explained with clarity and vitality.

entry.title: Nature: 7 Jul, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Particle physics isn’t going to die — even if the LHC finds no new particles
  • Europe must not backslide on climate action despite war in Ukraine
  • Blanket bans on fossil fuels hurt women and lower-income countries
  • Painkillers are dispensed less freely by night-shift doctors
  • ‘Smart’ clothing flexes to provide relief from the heat
  • Gut microbes that munch on orange pulp charge up metabolism
  • Even glaciers have a microbiome — including unique bacteria
  • ‘Ghost’ DNA from the world’s rarest wolves lingers in coyotes
  • Extreme heat already claims lives in Latin American cities — and the toll is set to rise
  • A space rock’s weird skips led to Earth’s biggest meteorite field
  • Biggest bacterium, comet capture and growing deserts
  • After Roe v. Wade: US researchers warn of what’s to come
  • Monkeypox in Africa: the science the world ignored
  • Fast-evolving COVID variants complicate vaccine updates
  • Mysterious child hepatitis continues to vex researchers
  • Rare ‘triple’ La Niña climate event looks likely — what does the future hold?
  • Did COVID vaccine mandates work? What the data say
  • Peter Higgs: the man behind the God particle
  • The plastic paradox, and how to regulate the seas: Books in brief
  • To end coal, adapt to regional realities
  • Emerging infectious diseases expose gaps in UN’s SDG framework
  • Accelerate US development of syphilis self-tests
  • Promote clean-energy transition in student education
  • The conference challenges faced by scientists who stutter
  • Need web data? Here’s how to harvest them
  • Improving livestock to fight poverty and empower women
  • Cancer cells spread aggressively during sleep
  • Topology turns the crank on a magnetoelectric switch
  • From the archive: Shakespeare’s stage directions, and fern fever
  • Touch-evoked itch pinned on Piezo1 ion-channel protein
  • Synthetic molecular cluster hints at mechanism of nitrogen fixation
  • A cell atlas for migraine research
  • The Higgs boson turns ten
  • Turbulent cold flows gave birth to the first quasars
  • A detailed map of Higgs boson interactions by the ATLAS experiment ten years after the discovery
  • A portrait of the Higgs boson by the CMS experiment ten years after the discovery
  • Entangling single atoms over 33 km telecom fibre
  • Direct observation of vortices in an electron fluid
  • Topologically protected magnetoelectric switching in a multiferroic
  • Nitrogen reduction by the Fe sites of synthetic [Mo3S4Fe] cubes
  • Optimization of avian perching manoeuvres
  • Whole-genome sequencing reveals host factors underlying critical COVID-19
  • PIEZO1 transduces mechanical itch in mice
  • Biosynthetic potential of the global ocean microbiome
  • Characterization and antiviral susceptibility of SARS-CoV-2 Omicron BA.2
  • Increased memory B cell potency and breadth after a SARS-CoV-2 mRNA boost
  • cBAF complex components and MYC cooperate early in CD8+ T cell fate
  • Ablation of cDC2 development by triple mutations within the Zeb2 enhancer
  • Deciphering the immunopeptidome in vivo reveals new tumour antigens
  • The metastatic spread of breast cancer accelerates during sleep
  • GREM1 is required to maintain cellular heterogeneity in pancreatic cancer
  • OCA-T1 and OCA-T2 are coactivators of POU2F3 in the tuft cell lineage
  • Compatibility rules of human enhancer and promoter sequences
  • eIF5B and eIF1A reorient initiator tRNA to allow ribosomal subunit joining
  • Cryo-EM structure of a type IV secretion system
  • A path forward for analysing the impacts of marine protected areas
  • Reply to: A path forward for analysing the impacts of marine protected areas
  • Events Guide 2022
Nature is a weekly international multidisciplinary scientific journal publishing peer-reviewed research in all fields of science and technology on the basis of its originality, importance, interdisciplinary interest, timeliness, accessibility, elegance and surprising conclusions. Nature also provides rapid, authoritative, insightful and arresting news and interpretation of topical and coming trends affecting science, scientists and the wider public.

entry.title: Poetry: July/August 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • Dear Reader,
  • we acknowledge ourselves
  • Hasísná
  • Emergence
  • hastiin k’aalógii ’ání
  • butterfly man tells a story
  • 1855
  • That you would be made of rock
  • sound installation
  • Did You Walk to Oklahoma?
  • Katuq.1
  • Examining the Phytolith

  • Cada día más cerca del fin del mundo

  • Influx—Reservation Lockdown
  • Creation
  • Grocery Story
  • Echolocations
  • Red Stick Noonshine
  • Love Shack, Anadarko
  • Lost Jewels
  • Finding Home
  • in new countries more eloquent than elsewhere
  • nothing as beautiful as gravity in the eternal folds
  • 34°54’15’’ N 111°47’30’’ W
  • Shipwreck Pastoral
  • Hotbeds in Norfolk, Virginia
  • Said the Tobacco to the Hand
  • The Shore of This Day
  • Floaters
  • Papyrus Pantoum
  • sáanii kǫ’ yee da’ałt’eis tł’óo’gi
  • Women are cooking outside over the fire
  • Grandma’s Challenge
  • In the City of Eve
  • Cousin Wolf Sings
  • same word in chickasaw for wolf and coyote
  • From the Roof Deck
  • maghrib
  • midnight
  • Learning about Treaties in School
  • Soft Thunder
  • Emerging
  • The Meadow Views: Sword and Symbolic History
  • Bead Workers
  • Bird Killer
  • Northbound #23 Bus (Old Indian Trail #2)

  • A White Man in the Audience Said I Owed Him
  • Wavelength // Waveless
  • Interrogation in a Nail Salon
  • Of My Body
  • 杨柳枝词
  • The Song of the Willow Branches
  • Field Notes
  • before the dark
  • Well-Traveled Path: An Interview with Arthur Sze
Founded in Chicago by Harriet Monroe in 1912, Poetry is the oldest monthly devoted to verse in the English-speaking world.

entry.title: TIME: July 4th, 2022 | Vol. 200, No. 1 | U.S.
entry.summary: TIME: July 4th, 2022 | Vol. 200, No. 1 | U.S. published at 2022-06-23 18:22PM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The Ocean Is Climate Change’s First Victim and Last Resort
  • The Motion of the Ocean Could Be the Next Big Source of Green Energy
  • We’re Gonna Need a Greener Boat
  • The Miraculous Trees That Could Save Pakistan’s Largest City from Climate Disaster
  • The Education of David Hogg
  • Schools Are Spending Billions on Safety Measures to Stop Mass Shootings. It’s Not Clear They Work
  • What a Bear Market Means for Consumers, Investors and the U.S. Economy
  • What You Need To Know About Colombia’s Election
  • Why the Bipartisan Push to Prevent Another Jan. 6 Is Running Out of Time
  • Moderna and Pfizer-BioNTech COVID-19 Vaccines Authorized for Children 6 Months and Older
  • The Rock Star Poet Playing Concerts to Ukrainians Sheltering Underground From Russian Bombs
  • Angelina Jolie: The World’s Refugee Crisis Is Bad Now—But It’s Only Going to Get Worse
  • Emmanuel Macron’s Uncertain Future After the French Elections
  • America’s Best Astrophysicists Are Taking UFOs Seriously. Maybe You Should Too
  • Disciplining Your Toddler at the End of the World
  • The Bittersweet Experience of Celebrating Pride in Florida
  • Anti-Abortion Pregnancy Centers Are Collecting Troves of Data That Could Be Weaponized Against Women
  • Senator Raphael Warnock Is Running Again for the Soul of Georgia
  • The Big Business of Being a Peloton Instructor
  • Elvis Still Has Not Left the Building
  • Maya Rudolph Deserves Better Than Loot‘s Toothless Billionaire Satire
Weekly US magazine.

entry.title: Wired Magazine: 23 Jun, 2022
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • The Loneliness of the Junior College Esports Coach
  • Here Comes the Sun—to End Civilization
  • When Covid Came for Provincetown
  • The Unwritten Laws of Physics for Black Women
  • Volodymyr Zelensky on War, Technology, and the Future of Ukraine
  • Six-Word Sci-Fi: Stories Written by You
  • This Designer Guitar Is Made From a Bent Sheet of Steel
  • A Photographer Captures Earth as a Strange New World
  • The Genre-Transcending Mastery of The Staircase
  • Life Is Great in the Age of No Secrets
  • Prediction Engines Are Like Karma: You Get What You Stream
  • The Multifarious Multiplexity of Taika Waititi
  • The ‘Form’ Element Created the Modern Web. Was It a Big Mistake?
  • ‘How Are They Weapons? That’s Only a Flashlight!’
  • Volodymyr Zelensky and the Art of the War Story
  • The US Military Is Building Its Own Metaverse
  • Bill Gates Is So Over This Pandemic
  • Can Social Media Be Redeemed?
  • Paradise at the Crypto Arcade: Inside the Web3 Revolution
  • Fast, Cheap, and Out of Control: Inside Shein’s Sudden Rise
  • With the Clock Running Out, Humans Need to Rethink Time Itself
  • Broken Charging Stations Could Be Stalling the EV Movement
  • Welcome to Janelle Monáe’s Dreamworld
  • Do I Really Need to Back Up All My Data?
  • The Intoxicating Pleasure of Conspiratorial Thinking
  • Extreme Heat Is a Disease for Cities. Treat It That Way
  • The End of Alcohol
  • Time Got So Much Weirder. The World Needs a New Lexicon
  • This Startup Wants to Get in Your Ears and Watch Your Brain
  • A Farmer’s Quest to Beat California’s Waves of Drought and Deluge
  • The Bitcoin Bust That Took Down the Web’s Biggest Child Abuse Site
  • A Million Little Pieces: The Race to Rebuild the World’s Reefs
  • If Someone Is Typing, Then Stops … Can I Ask Why?
  • I Finally Reached Computing Nirvana. What Was It All For?
  • The Enduring Legacy of Clubhouse’s Chatty Revolution
  • Is Russia’s Largest Tech Company Too Big to Fail?
  • It’s Like GPT-3, but for Code—Fun, Fast, and Full of Flaws
  • The Infinite Reach of Joel Kaplan, Facebook’s Man in Washington
  • ‘I’m the Operator’: The Aftermath of a Self-Driving Tragedy
  • Trapped in Silicon Valley’s Hidden Caste System
  • Zillow Botched Buying Homes. Opendoor Thinks It Can Do Better
  • The History—and Disturbing Resurrection—of Black Androids
  • Jacques Vallée Still Doesn’t Know What UFOs Are
  • The Best Way to Learn Online? Be a Lurker
  • Driving While Baked? Inside the High-Tech Quest to Find Out
  • Ada Palmer and the Weird Hand of Progress
  • How Telegram Became the Anti-Facebook
  • Do Good Doorbell Cams Make Good Neighbors?
  • 6 Apps That Take the Grind out of Your Workday
  • They Were ‘Calling to Help.’ Then They Stole Thousands
  • North Korea Hacked Him. So He Took Down Its Internet
  • Here Come the Underdogs of the Robot Olympics
  • Is It OK to Listen to a Butt-Dial Message?
  • This 22-Year-Old Builds Chips in His Parents’ Garage
  • A Grand Unified Theory of Buying Stuff
  • Can Being Reminded of My Death Improve My Life?
  • The Best of CES 2022
Wired is a full-color monthly American magazine, published in both print and online editions, that reports on how emerging technologies affect culture, the economy and politics. Monthly edition, best run at the start of every month.

entry.title: The Atlantic Magazine: July/August 2022
entry.summary: The Atlantic Magazine: July/August 2022 published at 2022-06-21 10:00AM.
entry.content:
Articles in this issue:
  • How Animals Perceive the World
  • Against Labels
  • American Rasputin
  • They Bent to Their Knees and Kissed the Sand
  • A Lost Trove of Civil War Gold, an FBI Excavation, and Some Very Angry Treasure Hunters
  • The Vindication of Jack White
  • We Have No Nuclear Strategy
  • My Daughter’s White Doll
  • The Book That Never Stops Changing
  • Beware the Luxury Beach Resort
  • White Author, Black Paragons
  • Why Is Dad So Mad?
  • Should We Blame Social Media for Society’s Divisions?
  • An Ode to My Thesaurus
  • Invitation
Current affairs and politics focussed on the US
